summary: I was born in Tunis, Tunisia in 1959, just a few years after Tunisia gained independence from France. Tunis is where my journey began, growing up in a family of four sons and my parents. Even though I left many times, Tunis always felt like home, and I returned there several times throughout my life, especially before moving permanently to the United States.

Tunisia is my homeland, where I was born and raised during a tough economic period after independence from France in 1956. Despite finishing my studies there in 1983 and working for a while, the economic situation pushed me to seek opportunities abroad. I returned to Tunisia multiple times, including in 1989 and 2007, when I married my high school sweetheart before moving permanently to the United States.

In 1981, I moved to France, specifically Paris, on a student visa to study mechanical engineering. I spent two years there before returning to Tunisia in 1983 to finish my studies. France was my first step abroad and marked the beginning of my long journey across several countries.

Paris was where I lived from 1981 to 1983 while studying mechanical engineering on a student visa. It was my first experience living outside Tunisia and gave me a taste of life in Europe. After two years in Paris, I returned home to complete my studies.

In 1985, I moved to Brussels, Belgium on a tourist visa to be with my friend Mohsan. I worked there for over two years, which was an important period for me as I tried to find better opportunities outside Tunisia. Brussels was a stepping stone before I moved to Norway.

In 1987, I moved to Oslo, Norway, after receiving an invitation from my brother Mohamed who was living there. I stayed for two years and even met a Norwegian woman I planned to marry, but the language barrier, weather, and cultural differences made it difficult to adapt. Eventually, I decided to return to Tunisia in 1989.

Norway was where I lived from 1987 to 1989, specifically in Oslo, thanks to my brother Mohamed who was a resident there. Although I hoped to settle down and marry, I struggled with the language and culture, which led me to return to Tunisia. Norway was an important chapter that taught me a lot about adapting to new environments.

In 1990, I obtained a Canadian tourist visa and moved there, spending about a year trying to get permanent residency. I stayed in Berkeley, California for six months during this period while waiting for my immigration application to be processed. Unfortunately, after two years of waiting and some friends betraying my trust, I was not able to settle in Canada.

Berkeley, California first became part of my life in 1991 when I stayed with my friend Lotfi for six months while trying to immigrate to Canada. After many years and travels, I returned to Berkeley in 2007 with my high school sweetheart, and we made it our permanent home. Today, I live there with my wife and two daughters, just a short distance from where I first stayed.

In 1995, after my Canadian immigration efforts failed, I moved to San Francisco on a tourist visa and stayed for five years. During that time, I worked and married an American woman, although our marriage ended after two years due to differences about starting a family. San Francisco was a significant place where I experienced both professional and personal growth.

The United States has been a major part of my life since 1991 when I first arrived in Berkeley. After several moves between Tunisia and the US, I finally settled permanently in 2007 with my wife and daughters in Berkeley, California. The US gave me opportunities to work, study, and build a family, and I consider myself a universal citizen because of my experiences here and abroad.
